descriptions.text-00: A citadel constructed entirely from the chitinous shells of giant insects, where metallic spider automatons serve as eternal guardians. Every doorway is cursed—passage through grants entry but extracts something intangible: a memory, a sense, a year of life. The architecture clicks and shifts as the shell-walls settle, and the spider guardians skitter through corridors repairing damage with secreted metal.

descriptions.text-01: In the husk of a dead age there stands a citadel of chitin and shell. No stone, no iron—only the armor of giants long since hunted, piled into leaning towers that creak like old bones. Within, the Iron Weavers keep their vigil. Spider-automatons of metal and wire, they crawl along the vaulted husk-walls, their many legs striking sparks, their bellies bleeding molten steel to seal each crack. To cross its thresholds is to be diminished. The doors open freely, but every passage takes its toll: a face forgotten, a sound forever silent, a year quietly cut from the span of a life. And as memories thin and senses fade, the fortress stirs and settles, its corridors clicking as if it remembers other shapes it once wore. So the citadel endures, fed by what it steals, while the Iron Weavers skitter in the dark, mending shell upon shell, until there is nothing left in the world that can still afford to enter.

descriptions.text-02: In the Age of the Great Swarm, the world was a chorus of clicking mandibles and chitinous plated gods. But time devours all, and now, only the Husk Citadel remains. A fortress raised not of stone, but of the molted carapaces of the Giants. Within its shifting halls, the Metal Weavers skitter—eternal sentinels, mending the fractures of the shell-walls with spun iron and cold devotion. But take heed, traveler. The thresholds here are not merely wood and hinge. They are hungry. To cross a doorway is to pay a toll... not of coin, but of essence. A cherished memory, the warmth of touch, a year of your spanning life... extracted in silence. Yes, indeed. The architecture breathes, and the Citadel settles. And those who seek to enter, must leave a piece of themselves behind.

descriptions.text-03: In the waning age of husks and hollowed empires, there stands the Carapace Citadel. It is raised not from stone but from the glazed shells of titanic insects, stacked and fused until they form a city of armor turned inward. Within, the Iron Spiders keep their endless watch. They are small, metallic things with needle limbs and furnace-bright eyes. They climb the shell-walls, stitch cracks with fresh metal, and spin bridges of wire over chasms that were not there the day before. Every threshold in the Carapace is a Witch-Door. To pass is to pay. Step through, and the door takes a memory, or a sense, or a single unseen year. If you pass often, you forget your own name. Because you forget, you cling to the Citadel’s few fixed words—Carapace, Iron Spider, Witch-Door—and those names become your whole world. The shell-walls never rest. They click, shift, and settle like teeth in a grinding jaw. Corridors bend where they once ran straight. Chambers close like fists. The Iron Spiders follow this slow movement, sealing, pruning, and mending so that the Citadel never cracks, only grows tighter. Those who live there trade door-crossings like coin: a memory for safety, a season for rank, a sense for one more chance to flee. If no one breaks the pattern, the Carapace will keep what it takes—years, senses, names—until there is nothing left outside it that remembers the world before the shells.

descriptions.text-04: In the silent era before the Second Molt, there stands The Carapace Citadel. It is not built of stone or wood. It is built of corpses. The walls are the severed plates of dead titans, stacked high and hollow against the gray sky. The architecture settles. The dry joints groan. The whole structure clicks in the dark. The Iron Weavers patrol the silence. They are clockwork arachnids, sleepless and cold. They vomit molten lead to seal the cracks in the chitin. If a wall fractures, a Weaver comes. Because the Weaver comes, the Citadel endures. To move deeper, you must cross The Threshing Thresholds. The doorways are hungry. They do not want gold. They want you. Pass the lintel, and feel the cut. A father’s name. The warmth of the sun. Ten years of blood. If you enter, you pay. Because you pay, you forget. Because you forget, you stay. The Weavers skitter. The walls click. The Citadel is not a fortress; it is a stomach that is slowly digesting your soul.

descriptions.text-05: In the dusk of a spent Age, when kingdoms had already gone to husk and hushed their banners, there was raised the Citadel of Carapace. It was fashioned not of stone but of the Sheaths of Colossal Insects, their hollow Shells stacked and fused into Tower and Hall, Wall and Gate. About its flanks the wind was forever thin and dry, and within its chambers there moved only the Iron Spiders, tireless Wardens whose brass limbs whispered over bone-pale floors. Its Passages were wrought as Trials, and its Doors were laid under ancient Malediction. No Threshold was crossed without Tribute. One Door would drink a Memory, leaving a Name half-forgotten. Another would take a Sense, dimming the world to ash. A third would steal away a single Year, quiet and unseen, from the sum of a mortal Life. Thus, Entry was always given, and something dear was always unmade. The Shell-Walls were never still. They settled and shifted with a slow, insect chattering, as if the slain Titans dreamed uneasily in their own remains. Where fissures opened, the Iron Spiders descended in glittering ranks, spinning black, gleaming Filaments—threads of cold Metal and curdled Ichor—to bind each wound shut. So the Citadel endured, ever-changing yet never falling, a Hive of Deathless Service and unending Cost. And now its Gates stand yet again ajar. The Curses have not faded; the Wardens have not slept. The next Footfall has been awaited, the next Offering weighed and measured in Silence. When you pass beneath that first low Arch, the Citadel will receive you as it has all others—without question, without mercy—and something that is yours, and only yours, shall be quietly taken.

descriptions.text-06: It was not raised by stone or mortar, but woven from the hollowed Carapaces of the Behemoths. The walls are observed to groan in a Restless Slumber, the dead plates shifting against one another, forever mended by the scuttling legs of the Iron Weavers. In the silence, the chittering of Cold Metal against Rotting Shell is the only prayer heard; the animate tending to the inanimate in an endless cycle of decay and renewal. Yet, the Gates are not barred to the pilgrim, for the Law of the Citadel requires no key, only a Tithe. To cross the Threshold is to be lightened of a burden one wishes to keep: a Cherished Memory, the Taste of Wine, or a Year of Breath stolen from one’s allotted span. Now the Great Mandibles part before you, and the Weavers cease their mending to watch. The way is open, but your soul feels heavier, and your past, already, begins to fade.

descriptions.text-07: Men spoke in whispers of the Citadel of Carapace, reared from the lacquered shells of monstrous beetles that had crawled in elder nights before the seas found their beds. No stone was set in its walls, but vast plates of blackened chitin, locked like the scales of some slain titan and studded with gray iron where tireless spider-things clung and toiled. Through its high doors a man might win entrance, yet never without payment; for the thresholds were accursed and drank of all who crossed them—a half-forgotten face, the savor of a long-loved song, a dwindling of the years in his bones. Ever the stronghold creaked and whispered in the night, its mailed flanks shifting as if the dead colossi yet stirred within, while along its dim corridors the iron arachnids scuttled, weeping threads of quicksilver steel to bind each rift and scar, that the chitinous fortress might endure when kingdoms were dust.

descriptions.text-08: There, rising from the waste like the severed claw of some dead god, stands the Citadel of Husks. It is a fortress not of stone, but of the iridescent, chitinous armor of behemoths slain in an Age undreamed of. Silence reigns here, broken only by the chittering tread of its guardians—mockery-spiders of cold iron and alchemical brass that stalk the gloom with sleepless, jeweled eyes. Beware the threshold, for every portal is a mouth that hungers. To pass beneath those arches is to barter with the void; the toll is not coin, but the soul’s own substance—a cherished memory snatched away, the dimming of the sight, or a year of life withered in a single heartbeat. And through the endless night, the citadel lives; the carapace-walls groan and click as they settle, while the iron weavers scuttle through the dark, mending the ancient scars with secretions of molten steel.

descriptions.text-09: In the wasted quarter of the world broods the Citadel of Carapace, a shell-born fortress named in caravan curses and deathbed whispers. Its walls are a mountain of fused beetle-plates and mantis-shields, lacquered in old iridescence, creaking as they settle like the armor of some drowsing colossus, while within its ribs the iron spiders keep their endless watch, jointed legs ticking like knives on stone and bellies heavy with cooling slag. Every gate and postern is a hungry mouth of chitin; to step through is to feel something bitten from you—a mother’s face blotted from memory, the taste of first snow turned to ash, the slow theft of a winter’s worth of breath—paid into the fortress like a tithe of the soul. The galleries shift and grind in the dark, stairs folding, corridors narrowing, shell-walls flexing like the plates of a living thorax, and the spider-wardens hurry along the seams to solder fresh wounds with gleaming spittle, stitching cracks, sealing blood, feeding the great husk so it may endure one more age. No one leaves it whole.

descriptions.text-10: Men speak in hushed tones of the Spire of the Shed Skin, that hollow mountain known only for its hunger and its clicking silence. It is no mason’s work of brick and mortar, but a carcass-tower stacked from the serrated plates of slain leviathans, a towering mosaic of iridescent black chitin and jagged horn that groans like a restless sleeper when the wind howls through its ribs. Within this breathing husk, the Weavers of Cold Iron—great, skittering constructs of gear and silver—scuttle endlessly across the carapace, weeping molten lead from their maws to suture the cracks where the great shells groan and settle against one another with the dry, clicking friction of dead bone. To cross a threshold here is to feed the Spire’s starving belly, for every door demands a toll of the soul, stripping the flesh from the spirit: a mother’s face suddenly erased from memory, the taste of wine vanishing forever from the tongue, or the spine curving instantly under the weight of a stolen decade. The toll is paid in silence.

descriptions.text-11: Upon the husk-strewn plain rises the Carapace Citadel, a many-jointed stronghold wrought from titanic beetle-shells and locust-carapaces, lacquer-dull and amber-glossed, clamped together with blackened brass and marrow-pale rivets. Within its ribbed and crawling halls, bronze-limbed, jewel-eyed spider-sentinels creep in tireless circuits, their needle-feet ticking like distant teeth, their bellies sweating quicksilver metal to seal each crack and wound in the living walls. For every gate is a hungry mouth and every threshold a covetous altar: to step beneath those chitin-arched jaws is to be weighed and tasted, and in payment the house takes what it desires—a sunlit memory, a long-beloved sense, a slow-burning year torn invisibly from the marrow of your days. And as you pass on, the citadel settles and shudders and reknits itself around the loss it has devoured, chattering softly in shell and iron, as though the whole grim edifice were savoring your squandered life like a slow-dissolving sweet.

descriptions.text-12: A citadel rose out of the waste like the afterthought of some titanic insect, a fortress cobbled together from the lacquered shells of giants long since cracked and emptied. From its lowest, cramped culverts to the loftiest, airless parapets, the walls were a mosaic of chitin: knuckled ridges of thorax, overlapping fans of wing-case, joint upon joint, as though a million ruined carapaces had been persuaded into the shape of a city and then denied the right to move again. Within, the passages were never still. The very fabric of the place adjusted itself with a dry, perpetual patience. Plates of shell settled upon one another with a faint, teeth-on-metal clicking, a distant crepitation that ran along the corridors like the murmur of an enormous insect asleep, yet dreaming of movement. Through this shifting husk went the spider-guardians: a race of jointed metals that gleamed dully in the half-light, their narrow limbs articulating with prim exactitude. They skittered on their filigree legs, pausing to nose at a fracture, to drool a bright thread of cooling metal into a crack, to polish away a scar in the armour of the walls as though mending some wounded, beloved monster. Every doorway was a mouth and every mouth exacted a toll. To pass beneath those chitinous lintels was to feel the air lean in, inquisitive and cold, and to surrender something that had never before seemed separable: the taste of a certain winter morning, the exact colour of a parent’s eyes, the steady weight of a single year from the spine of one’s life. These losses were not named. They went wordlessly into the citadel. But somewhere behind the layered husks and the industrious, tinkering spiders, the place grew heavier with them, as if the memories and hours and senses it had swallowed were slowly ripening in the dark.

descriptions.text-13: The citadel did not so much rise from the earth as encrust it—a vast, iridescent scab composed entirely of the hollowed, carapace-walls of forgotten leviathans. It was a architecture of anatomy, a vertical graveyard of polished chitin where the sunlight struck the beetle-black ramparts and died there, swallowed by the dull, purple sheen of the walls. Through these segmented corridors, where the silence was not an absence of sound but a heavy, brooding presence, moved the custodians. They were arachnids of cold iron and ticking brass; they skittered with a terrifying, jerky precision, weeping molten alloy from their spinnerets to bind the fractures where the great shells groaned and settled against one another with the dry, clicking friction of dead bone. Yet the true malignancy of the place lay in its thresholds. To pass beneath the lintels of this place was to submit to a spiritual usury, for the doorways were thieves. They demanded a toll of the soul's coinage—a sudden graying of the hair, the theft of a childhood memory, or the dulling of a sense—leaving the traveler to step into the next room lighter, older, and haunted by the ghost of what they had left behind in the archway.

descriptions.text-14: A citadel rises there, wrought entire from the husks of insects vast beyond dreaming, their carapaces fitted one against another like the interlocking memories of some dead god. The walls possess the colour of old amber where the light troubles them, and of dried blood where it does not. Through corridors curved as the innards of great beetles, metallic spiders process in their eternal circuits—guardians forged from secreted silver, their legs describing small genuflections upon the chitin floors with each measured step, a sound like the ticking of a thousand ruined clocks. No doorway stands innocent here. Each threshold drinks of those who cross it, supping upon some essence the traveller cannot name until it is missed: a year drawn thin as silk from the spool of remaining life, or else a memory—perhaps of a mother's voice, perhaps of summer—dissolved into the architrave like salt into dark water. The walls settle and resettle with clicks and groans, for the citadel yet lives in its fashion, its shell-plates grinding one upon another as though the whole vast carcass were attempting, still, to breathe. And where fractures web across the amber surfaces, the spider-guardians gather in their patient multitudes, seeping molten metal from their thoraxes, sealing each wound with the devotion of priests attending to a god who will not die.

descriptions.text-15: The citadel squats on the plain like something that died halfway through metamorphosis and decided not to finish, its walls a layered mosaic of insect shell: lacquered plates the colour of old honey, dull wing-veins milkily translucent, mandibles frozen into grimacing cornices. When the wind comes it does not whistle; it rattles the chitin so that the whole structure answers with a faint, dry clicking, like teeth worrying a secret in the dark. The place seems to be listening. It seems to be waiting. Each doorway is cut from a single piece of carapace, its surface polished to a soft, uneven gloss that holds a distorted reflection—faces stretched long, eyes misplaced, a shoulder where the mouth should be. Step close and there is the smell of warm metal and shed skin, that faint sweetness you find in an empty wasp nest. People who pass through do so quickly, not because the archways are narrow, but because the threshold feels like a wet tongue pressed flat, tasting for something it intends to keep. No one can quite say what is taken; they only notice, later, that a beloved song has lost its last verse in their mind, or that winter no longer feels cold enough, or that a single grey hair has appeared where none was before. Inside, the shell-walls never quite rest. Plates flex almost imperceptibly, ridges rise and subside, seams click against one another with the soft, patient sound of counting beads. The corridors realign by degrees, turning corners where there were none, closing off alcoves as though the citadel were folding its limbs in closer. Along these shifting joints move the spider guardians: jointed metal bodies the size of dogs, their legs tapping out a measured, metallic staccato, their abdomens dragging fine threads of quicksilver that harden into new rivets and braces. They work without hurry, smoothing cracks, sealing fractures, stitching fresh armour onto their slumbering host. When one pauses, its many-faceted eyes catch what little light there is and hold it, not shining but hoarding, as if even brightness here must pay a toll to stay.

descriptions.text-16: The Citadel does not merely stand; it huddles against the horizon, a vast and segmented carapace of black-gold iridescence that seems to resent the sunlight. The walls are not masonry, but a mosaic of stolen armors, fused plates of giant chitin that sweat a faint, acrid oil when the heat rises, shimmering with the heavy, bruised purple of a beetle’s wing. It is a structure that never truly sleeps. Deep within the curved hallways, the architecture groans as it settles, a sound of friction and dry longing, like a thousand dry knuckles cracking in unison. Through these ribbed arteries, the guardians skitter. They are ticking things, arachnid automatons of brass and wire that move with a terrifying, liquid grace, their needle-feet tapping out a chaotic Morse code on the shell-floor. They are the nurses of this dead place. Where a wall fractures or a plate peels back, they swarm, mandibles working furiously to suture the wound, vomiting hot, silver solder that hisses as it cools, binding the organic husk with veins of gleaming metal. But the danger lies in the thresholds. The doorways here are not passive gaps in the wall; they are hungry mouths that demand a toll for the privilege of transit. To cross a threshold is to feel a sudden, violent hook inside the chest, a spiritual theft that leaves no scar but creates a hollow. You step through an archway of serrated bone, and the air grows instantly colder. You reach for the memory of your mother’s voice, and find only silence. The Citadel takes what it wishes—the taste of an apple, the memory of snow, the strength in your left hand—and digests it into the mortar, leaving you lighter, lesser, and allowed to proceed.

descriptions.text-17: The citadel breathes in its own fashion—a slow, arthritic settling of shell-walls the colour of burnt honey, each plate once the flank or thorax of something vast and segmented that died in an age no one remembers clearly. It is a jealous place. It resents stillness, and so the corridors click and groan at odd hours, chitin sliding against chitin with a sound like knuckles cracking in an empty cathedral, the whole structure shifting a fraction of an inch as though trying to scratch an itch it cannot name. Through these halls the spider guardians move, eight-legged and silent save for the soft tap of metal on shell. Their bodies gleam the colour of old pewter, and where the walls have cracked or splintered they pause, patient as monks, and weep bright filaments of secreted metal into the wounds until the breach seals over, leaving seams that catch torchlight like veins of silver in ore. They do not sleep. They do not speak. They have been mending this place longer than anyone has been alive to thank them. Every threshold here is hungry. Step through a doorway and you will arrive where you intended—that much the citadel permits—but something stays behind: the scent of your mother's hearth, perhaps, or the precise sound of your own name spoken by someone who loved you, or a winter you had forgotten you'd survived. You will not notice the loss at first. You will only feel, later, a small hollow where something used to fit. The citadel collects these payments and keeps them somewhere in its clicking heart, and no one has yet discovered what it does with them, or why it is still so hungry after all these years.

descriptions.text-18: The citadel rises like a calcified exoskeleton that refused to die, a colossal husk of interlocking plates and fused carapaces, their lacquered surfaces burnished by ages of abrasive winds until they gleam with the subdued luster of old fingernails; sunlight strikes its ramparts and is swallowed, snuffed along ridges of ridged chitin where shadows nest in the sutures and never quite unclench. Each tower spirals upward in segmented spirals, molted from the memory of the titanic insects whose shells now serve as ribs and vertebrae; the walls do not stand so much as brace themselves, creaking almost imperceptibly, flexing with a dry, incremental friction that runs through the structure like the grinding of distant teeth. Within, the corridors narrow and widen in a slow peristalsis, their vaulted ceilings bowing by fractions of an inch, making the air feel compacted and reused, as though every breath were borrowed from some older, more patient inhabitant of the shell. Every doorway is an orifice that knows you, framed with overlapping mandible-arches and filigreed spurs, beautiful as enamel inlaid with onyx and yet faintly moist at the seams, as if something inside still considered the possibility of closing forever; to cross a threshold is to feel a slight, inaudible suturing in the chest as it takes its toll—an unnoticed memory unspooling, a color leached from one’s private spectrum, a year quietly sheared from the spine of one’s remaining time. Passing through, you sense not a curse pronounced but a transaction completed, the door’s invisible mouth tasting what it has taken, the loss echoing as a thin, metallic aftertaste at the back of the mind. The sound of these transactions accumulates in the halls as a kind of negative music, a hush so dense it feels layered, like overlapping wings folded over a heartbeat. Along the corridors—arteries lined with overlapping plates that click softly as they settle—the spider guardians advance and retreat in endless patrol, their bodies an intricate alloy of filigreed brass and blackened iron, legs jointed with obscene grace. They move with jerky precision that nonetheless resolves, over distance, into a kind of grim choreography: each articulated limb tapping out a staccato rhythm against the chitin, a rapid morse of impact and recoil that sets faint tremors through the walls, as though the citadel were listening to its own pulse. When they find a crack, a flake, a fault-line where the shell-structure has begun to delaminate, they halt with marionette abruptness; abdomens tilt, mandible-nozzles unfurl, and threads of molten metal drip with viscous deliberation, hissing softly as they fuse to the old chitin like fresh scar tissue knitting over an ancient wound. The smell is sweet and acrid at once, like hot coins pressed into resin, and the sound of cooling alloy—a minuscule tick, tick, tick—travels down the hallways in brittle waves, until the whole citadel seems to be quietly resetting its bones.

descriptions.text-19: The citadel does not merely stand upon the horizon but rather crouches, a vast and segmented accumulation of iridescent carapaces that heave with the slow, tectonic breath of the dormant dead, their burnished surfaces swallowing the weak sunlight that dares to touch the ramparts only to fracture and die within the deep, oily fissures of the walls. Through the aortic corridors, where the air hangs thick with the thrumming resonance of enclosed silence, prowl the custodians: clockwork arachnids of filigreed silver and rusted iron that move with a terrifying, staccato precision, their multi-jointed limbs picking across the uneven floor as they vomit streams of molten alloy to suture the cracking exoskeleton of the keep. To traverse the threshold of any archway is to submit to a spiritual vivisection, for the architecture is gluttonous, demanding a tithe of intimate warmth—a cherished memory, the vibrance of the color blue, or the weight of a decade’s life—siphoned away in a rush of cold air as the heavy, chitinous gates shudder and click shut behind the hollowed traveler.

descriptions.text-20: The citadel breathes in the manner of all dead things that refuse stillness—a slow, tectonic respiration of interlocking carapaces, each shell-segment fitted against its neighbor with the intimacy of knucklebones. Walls of burnished chitin rise in sweeping parabolas, their surfaces scarred by the death-throes of the colossal arthropods from which they were harvested, and where dawn light strikes these ramparts it shatters into opalescent ribbons before drowning in the material's deeper strata, swallowed into browns and blacks that hold no memory of the sun. The architecture settles ceaselessly: a dry-friction whisper of plate against plate, a pullulation of clicks and groans that rolls through the corridors like the digestive murmur of some vast, slumbering gut. Through these passages move the spider-automatons—eight-limbed and elegant in their grotesquerie, their segmented legs articulating with jerky, staccato precision as they traverse walls and ceilings with equal indifference to gravity. They are beautiful in the way scalpels are beautiful: lustrous thoraxes of hammered bronze, mandibles that flex and part with surgical delicacy, eyes like clustered garnets catching torchlight and holding it, jealous and unblinking. Where the shell-walls crack or splinter, the spiders gather in skittering congregations, their spinnerets extruding threads of molten metal that cool into sutures of silver and copper—wounds stitched shut with the patience of centuries. And the doorways—ah, the doorways are where the citadel sups. Each threshold drinks something from those who cross it: the weight of a year, perhaps, or the sound of a mother's voice, or the precise blue of a sky seen once in childhood and never again. You will not feel it go. You will only notice, later, that something has grown quieter inside you, that some room in your mind stands empty where furniture once was. The citadel grows fat on such absences. The Tenders click their approval. And the walls settle deeper, content, waiting for the next passage.

descriptions.text-21: At a distance it seems less built than shed, a vast carapace discarded by something that decided it no longer needed shelter. Plates of old amber and smoke-dark varnished bone rise into the light, overlapping like frozen waves, yet they never quite hold still: the whole citadel gives a slow, insect breath, creaking inward, sighing outward, a patient grind of seams settling and resettling in their own remembered shapes. When the wind moves across it, the shell-walls answer with a faint ticking—as if a million dead mandibles still test the air, still whisper their refusal to be entirely gone. Only when you come close do you notice the thresholds. They gape as simple openings at first, their rims rubbed to something like polished horn, dull as dried honey and old teeth. Crossing one feels like stepping through a held breath: the chitin tightens, there is a soft internal clink, and something inside you is quietly poured out, decanted like wine you did not agree to share. A scent of dusted incense and stale nectar clings there, and each passage leaves you a fraction thinner—one memory slightly blurred at the edges, a taste gone from your tongue, a year of your future folded away and filed somewhere deep within the walls’ layered rind. The citadel drinks in this payment without comment, its corridors lengthening by the width of a heartbeat, its shadows thickening like congealed, dried blood. Within, the guardians move. They arrive first as a faint tapping, a bead of rhythm on the floor—then as a scatter of reflections, then finally as shapes: metallic spiders, all jointed devotion and needle-fine legs, pacing their patrols with the somber precision of cloistered priests. Their bodies glint like tarnished silver chalices overturned, their eyes a dull, old-coin gleam that never quite meets yours. When the living architecture shifts too sharply and a shell-plate cracks, they hurry to the wound, bowing and rising in tireless genuflections. From their articulated abdomens they exude thin threads of quickmetal, bright as fresh cut vein-dark ore; the substance steams, hisses, and then cools, knitting broken chitin with the efficiency of scar tissue. So the citadel feeds and is fed, vast as a dead god’s ribcage and yet attended by these tiny, ceaseless hands, each scrape and click and whispered drip echoing some private loss that no one will notice until it is already gone.

descriptions.text-22: It rises not in stone, but in a series of serrated gasps, a vast, vertical accumulation of fused carapace gleaming in shades of bruised lantern-light and fossilized honey. The structure does not merely stand; it settles, the tectonic plates of the walls grinding against one another with the dry, rhythmic clicking of a thousand knuckles cracking in the dark, a restless slumber that suggests the architecture is waiting for a pulse. Skittering through these hollow veins are the custodians, articulated deacons of iron and oil who perform their endless genuflections upon the walls, weeping molten solder to seal the cracks in the master’s shell. They move with a terrifying, clicking piety, indifferent to the intruders who wander below, lost in the digestive gloom. To cross a threshold here is to be tasted. Every archway is a hungry mouth that demands a tithe for passage, supping not on flesh, but on the intangible drift of the soul—the sudden, silent evaporation of a mother’s face from your memory, or the warmth of a childhood summer—swallowed whole by the jambs, leaving you lighter, colder, and forever allowed inside.

descriptions.text-23: The citadel breathes. Not as living things breathe, but as old wounds do—a slow, involuntary settling of matter that was never meant to hold this shape. Its walls are carapace, the hollowed husks of insects whose names have been forgotten by everything except the dust, and they arc upward in great curved ramparts the colour of dried blood and old amber, of bruises gone yellow at the edges. When the wind finds the right angle, the whole structure groans and clicks, shell grinding against shell in a language that sounds almost like hunger. Through its corridors move the Tenders—eight-legged priests of iron and blackened copper who skitter and pause, skitter and pause, their movements a kind of genuflection to architecture they alone understand. Where the walls crack, they gather. Where the chitin splits and whistles, they press their hinged bodies close and weep bright metal from spinnerets hidden in their thoraxes, sealing wounds with secretions that cool to the colour of tarnished silver. They have served since before memory, and they will serve after it, and they ask nothing in return except that you do not interfere with their devotions. But the doorways—ah, the doorways are where the citadel sups. Each threshold drinks something from those who cross it: the weight of a year, perhaps, or the sound of a mother's voice, or the precise blue of a sky seen once in childhood and never again. You will not feel it go. You will only notice, later, that something has grown quieter inside you, that some room in your mind stands empty where furniture once was. The citadel grows fat on such absences. The Tenders click their approval. And the walls settle deeper, content, waiting for the next passage.
